title: CKA Knowledge Test
description: Test your understanding of CKA with 25 questions.

questions.question: In a 9-node Kubernetes cluster hosting multiple teams, you need strict isolation and predictable budgets. Outline a concrete plan to implement per-namespace quotas and limits, enable Pod Security Standards in restricted mode, and validate with live workloads. Include exact manifests, the commands to apply them, and how you verify enforcement and absence of privilege escalation during rollouts?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical security hardening and multi-tenant governance in a production Kubernetes cluster.

## Key Concepts
- Pod Security Standards
- ResourceQuota and LimitRange
- Admission control and namespace isolation
- Verification via events and can-i checks

## Code Example
```yaml
# team-a ResourceQuota
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-a-quota
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
```

```yaml
# team-a LimitRange
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: team-a-limits
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: 2Gi
    min:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: 128Mi
    type: Container
```

```yaml
# Privileged pod (test, denied under restricted)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: privileged-test
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  containers:
  - name: test
    image: alpine
    command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 5"]
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: true
      privileged: true
```

Follow-up questions:
- How would you audit namespace policy drift over time?
- How would you roll back quotas without impacting running pods?

Audit RBAC bindings across all namespaces to identify any cluster-wide admin ClusterRoleBindings that may be inadvertently applying to prod workloads. Reproduce the issue in a test namespace to confirm the root cause. C…

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to recognize memory pressure as a failure mode and perform reproducible debugging with kubectl, especially for beginners learning OOMKilled signals and resource tuning.

## Key Concepts
- Pod memory pressure and OOMKilled
- Resource requests/limits and horizontal pod autoscaling
- Observability with kubectl describe, events, top, and metrics-server
- Safe rollout of resource changes

## Code Example
```bash
kubectl get deploy
kubectl get pods
kubectl describe pod <pod>
kubectl get events --sort-by=".lastTimestamp"
kubectl top pod
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate that the new limits prevent future spikes without over-provisioning?
- What are the trade-offs of increasing limits vs adding a limit-range or vertical pod autoscaler?

questions.question: A StatefulSet running a partitioned Kafka cluster on a CSI-enabled PVC experiences a rolling update where a broker pod is stuck Terminating due to its PVC detaching slowly. Explain the root cause, how you would diagnose, and concrete steps to safely complete the rollout without data loss, including finalizers, detachment, and StorageClass settings?

Check PVC status with kubectl get pvc -n ns; describe pvc to see Pending reason; list PVs to find a binding. If none, create a Static PV and bind, or install a StorageClass and patch the PVC storageCl

Start by collecting pod events and logs: kubectl describe pod <pod>; kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> --previous; kubectl top pod <pod>. If memory overuse, patch the Deployment with resources: reques

Root cause is CSI detachment race leaving the PVC bound; fix by auditing PVC/PV status, finalizers, and CSI logs. Diagnose with kubectl describe pod/pvc, kubectl get pvc/pv, and events for detach erro

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical understanding of PVC lifecycle, CSI detachment, finalizers, and StatefulSet rollouts.

## Key Concepts
- PVC/PV lifecycle and detach
- CSI driver detach mechanics
- StatefulSet rollout semantics
- pvc-protection finalizer and reclaimPolicy

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudocode for detection and remediation
if (pod.status?.phase === 'Terminating' && pvc.finalizers?.includes('kubernetes.io/pvc-protection')) {
  patchPVCFinalizers(pvc, []);
  deletePod(pod);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you verify data safety during detach?
- How would you adjust StorageClass/CSI settings to prevent future stalls?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of admission webhooks, injection correctness, and safe rollback procedures under production pressure.

## Key Concepts
- Mutating admission webhooks and sidecar injection
- API server and webhook logging for traceability
- Controlled repro in a test namespace with concurrency limits
- Safe rollback/patch strategies with end-to-end validation

## Code Example
```bash
# List affected deployments and their pods in prod
kubectl get deploy -n prod -o wide
kubectl get pods -n prod -o wide | grep -i CrashLoopBackOff

# Inspect webhook and API server events
kubectl logs -n kube-system deploy/kube-apiserver-<node> | tail -n 200
kubectl logs -n kube-system deploy/wh-svc-webhook
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you sandbox a hotfix to avoid recurrence without impacting users?
- What metrics would you monitor post-remediation to confirm stability?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical debugging of a low-level configuration mismatch that causes runtime errors, a common beginner pain point.

## Key Concepts

- Service port vs container port mapping
- kubectl describe/get/patch and rollout verification
- Endpoints and traffic routing health

## Code Example

```bash
# Check service and endpoints
kubectl describe svc api
kubectl get endpoints api

# Align ports (example patch)
kubectl patch svc api -p '{"spec":{"ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8080}]}}'
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you validate the fix in a staging environment?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to diagnose intermittent webhook failures in a live cluster and balance safety with rapid fixes.

## Key Concepts
- MutatingAdmissionWebhook behavior and lifecycle
- namespaceSelector rules and safe disabling strategies
- apiserver logs and webhook health monitoring
- kubectl events and pod creation flow
- Safe rollback and recovery procedures

## Code Example
```bash
# Inspect webhook configuration
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfiguration <name> -o yaml

# Minimal patch to exclude namespace
kubectl patch mutatingwebhookconfiguration <name> --type merge -p '{
  "webhooks": [{
    "name": "sidecar-injector",
    "namespaceSelector": {
      "matchExpressions": [{
        "key": "kubernetes.io/metadata.name",
        "operator": "NotIn",
        "values": ["target-namespace"]
      }]
    }
  }]
}'

# Create test pod to verify fix
kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx -n target-namespace
```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a comprehensive audit and containment strategy with specific implementation details and validation methods.

## Key Concepts
- Kubernetes audit policy configuration
- API server audit logging parameters
- Log aggregation and SIEM integration
- RBAC containment strategies
- NetworkPolicy-based isolation
- Audit log validation and monitoring

## Code Example
```yaml
# audit-policy.yaml example
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
- level: Metadata
  namespaces: ["kube-system", "kube-public"]
- level: Request
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["pods", "secrets", "serviceaccounts"]
  - group: "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    resources: ["roles", "rolebindings"]
```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests expertise in PVC binding, quotas, and CSI integration under real-world constraints. Emphasizes reproducibility, safe rollback, and validation with data-plane tests.

## Key Concepts

- PVC binding lifecycle and VolumeBinding phase
- Namespace quotas and their impact on dynamic provisioning
- CSI driver behavior and logs debugging
- Safe rollback and canary validation in stateful apps

## Code Example

```bash
# Identify affected PVCs and pods
kubectl get pvc -n cache-prod
kubectl get pods -n cache-prod -l app=redis

# Inspect details and quota
kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name> -n cache-prod
kubectl describe quota -n cache-prod

# Reproduce in test namespace
kubectl create namespace test-scenario
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you monitor quota violations at scale?
- How would you prevent quota-induced binding failures in future deployments?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical debugging of image pull failures in a beginner-friendly way, focusing on kubectl basics.

## Key Concepts

- ImagePullBackOff and private registry access
- imagePullSecrets and ServiceAccount bindings
- kubectl describe, kubectl get events, kubectl patch
- Verifying and updating secrets without downtime
- Rollout restart and validation basics

## Code Example

```javascript
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
kubectl get serviceaccount default -o yaml
kubectl get secret <secret-name> -o yaml
kubectl patch serviceaccount default -p '{"imagePullSecrets":[{"name":"<secret-name>"}]}'
kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name>
kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you verify the registry is reachable from cluster nodes?
- How would you test image pull before a full rollout?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests disaster-recovery skills on control plane with minimal downtime.

## Key Concepts
- etcd backup/restore, member lifecycle, and control‑plane manifests
- API server availability validation and data-consistency checks

## Code Example
```bash
# health
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \\ --cacert /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.pem \\ --cert /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.pem \\ --key /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server-key.pem
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test DR in a staging cluster?
- What if restore diverges from the current member set?

questions.question: In a cluster using a MutatingAdmissionWebhook secured by TLS, the webhook's certs and CA bundle are rotated and stored in a Secret. Outline a concrete, minimally disruptive plan to diagnose (kubectl get MutatingWebhookConfiguration -o yaml; inspect caBundle), verify the TLS chain from inside a pod, and implement a fix (synchronize caBundle with the CA, reissue certs, or roll out the webhook deployment) with verification steps?

Inspect token projection and expiry: kubectl get pod <pod> -o yaml | grep -A5 projected; fetch token: kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token; decode expiry: echo

Plan: verify both the Secret volume and EnvFrom usage, confirm live credential paths, and simulate rotation to observe runtime behavior. Implement a minimal auto-reload by adding a sidecar that watche

**Diagnosis Phase** 1. Retrieve the current webhook configuration: ```bash kubectl get MutatingWebhookConfiguration <webhook-name> -o yaml ``` 2. Extract and inspect the `caBundle` field from the output (Base64-encoded)…

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests practical knowledge of webhook certificate rotation, CA trust management across Kubernetes components, and safe zero-downtime rollout procedures in production environments.

## Key Concepts

- **MutatingAdmissionWebhook**: Kubernetes admission controller that intercepts create, update, and delete operations before objects are persisted to etcd. It can modify the incoming object (mutating) or reject the request entirely.

- **caBundle**: Base64-encoded CA certificate bundle embedded in the MutatingWebhookConfiguration. The Kubernetes API server uses this to verify the webhook server's certificate when forwarding admission requests.

- **Certificate Rotation**: In production environments, certificates must be rotated periodically. When the CA in the Secret changes, the caBundle must be updated to maintain trust.

- **Rolling Updates**: Deployments should use rolling updates to ensure zero downtime during certificate rotation or configuration changes.

## Common Pitfalls

- Forgetting to update caBundle after CA rotation
- Using mismatched Certs between Secret and webhook configuration
- Not restarting webhook pods after certificate changes
- Failing to verify the fix before considering the incident resolved

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests practical Kubernetes debugging: diagnosing NetworkPolicy egress, DNS reachability, and safe policy patching.

## Key Concepts
- NetworkPolicy; egress rules; selectors; DNS resolution
- Pod labeling; policy scope; minimal change strategy
- Debug workflows with ephemeral pods and end-to-end verification

## Code Example
```bash
kubectl get netpol -n payments -o yaml
kubectl get pods -n payments -o wide
kubectl run -n payments dbg --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl:8.2 --command -- sh
curl -v https://gateway.api:443
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you prevent regressions when updating NetPols in production?
- What observability would you add to detect similar issues earlier?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Diagnoses real-world DaemonSet rollout issues, canary upgrades, and node-local debugging with minimal downtime. Tests troubleshooting, Kubernetes familiarity, and practical logging validation.

## Key Concepts
- DaemonSet upgrade strategy and canaries
- Node-level debugging of hostPath mounted logs
- InitContainers and startup guards
- End-to-end log shipping verification

## Code Example
```javascript
// Example: guard for missing log path in startup
if (!fs.existsSync('/var/log/containers')) {
  fs.mkdirSync('/var/log/containers', { recursive: true });
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you automate the canary upgrade and rollback?
- What metrics would you monitor during the rollout?

questions.question: In a Kubernetes cluster, a Deployment with 3 replicas serves a REST API behind a ClusterIP Service. After a ConfigMap update, requests intermittently return 502s while pods stay Ready. The API reads a feature flag from the ConfigMap at startup. questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical Kubernetes troubleshooting: ConfigMaps, volume mounts, and rollout flow, plus concrete commands.

## Key Concepts
- ConfigMap keys and volume mounts
- Pod describe, events, and rollout restart
- Verifying service endpoints and basic load verification

## Code Example
```bash
kubectl get cm app-config -o yaml
kubectl describe pod <pod> | grep -i ConfigMap
kubectl patch cm app-config -p '{"data":{"FEATURE_FLAG":"on"}}'
kubectl rollout restart deploy api
``` 
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle multiple ConfigMaps or key renames without downtime?
- What are pitfalls if the config key is read only at startup?

questions.question: Your 6-node Kubernetes cluster must forward audit events to an external SIEM via a webhook sink. Detail the exact YAMLs and kubectl commands to enable an API-server audit policy, configure a canary webhook backend with retry and rate-limiting, perform a canary rollout, verify delivery to the SIEM, and implement a rollback if delivery stalls?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Auditing is central to security and compliance; this tests practical setup of an external webhook sink with a canary rollout and safe rollback procedures.

## Key Concepts
- Kubernetes Audit policy configuration
- AuditSink and webhook sink implementation
- Canary rollout strategy and rollback mechanisms
- Retry logic, exponential backoff, and rate limiting

## Code Example
```yaml
# audit-policy.yaml
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
- level: Metadata
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["pods","configmaps","secrets"]
```

```yaml
# audit-sink.yaml (canary sink)
apiVersion: auditregistration.k8s.io/v1alpha1
kind: AuditSink
metadata:
  name: audit-sink-canary
spec:
  webhook:
    clientConfig:
      service:
        name: audit-webhook-service
        namespace: audit-system
        path: "/audit"
      timeout: 30s
    throttle:
      qps: 10
      burst: 20
    retryBackoff:
      base: 1s
      max: 30s
```

```bash
# Apply audit policy and canary sink
kubectl apply -f audit-policy.yaml
kubectl apply -f audit-sink-canary.yaml

# Generate test events
kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx --restart=Never
kubectl delete pod test-pod

# Verify SIEM ingestion and proceed to full rollout
curl -X POST https://siem-endpoint/audit/check
kubectl apply -f audit-sink-full.yaml
```

To debug OOMKilled pods in an EKS cluster during a surge, follow this structured approach: **1. Immediate Diagnosis** - Identify failing pods: `kubectl get pods -n <namespace> --field-selector=status.phase=Failed` - Exa…

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests practical incident response in RBAC-heavy clusters, emphasizing quick containment, credential hygiene, and controlled re‑privileging.

## Key Concepts
- RBAC, least privilege, and binding scoping
- Incident response and credential rotation
- Namespace isolation and network policies
- Cluster audit and verification

## Code Example
```bash
# Identify risky bindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o yaml
kubectl get rolebindings -A -o yaml
# Revoke elevated access
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding admin-binding
# Rotate SA tokens
kubectl delete secret <sa-token-*> -n prod
kubectl delete sa <sa> -n prod
kubectl create sa <sa> -n prod
# Apply network policy
kubectl apply -f policy.yaml
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you audit for token leakage in CI pipelines?
- What invisible costs could arise from rapid RBAC revocation, and how would you mitigate them?

questions.question: In a 3-node cluster, Namespace prod hosts a Deployment 'web-app' with 6 replicas. Each pod requests 200m CPU and 256Mi memory. Namespace prod has ResourceQuota: CPU 1, Memory 1Gi. New pods fail to schedule while existing run. Outline exact kubectl steps to verify quota enforcement, identify Pending pods, and implement a minimal fix (scale down the deployment by 2 or reduce requests) with verification?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests knowledge of Kubernetes scheduling and quota enforcement, and how to safely adjust live workloads without violating constraints.

## Key Concepts
- ResourceQuota; - Pending vs Running; - Pod resources requests/limits; - Scaling and patching deployments

## Code Example
```yaml
# Example patch to reduce requests
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 150m
            memory: 256Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 300m
            memory: 512Mi
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you prevent this in the future (quota-aware autoscaling)?
- What metrics would you monitor to detect quota pressure early?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked
This question evaluates the candidate's ability to diagnose and resolve storage-related issues in production Kubernetes environments. It tests practical knowledge of CSI driver interaction, PVC expansion workflows, and the ability to implement safe rollback or canary deployment strategies when issues arise from infrastructure policy changes.

## Key Concepts
- **PVC expansion flow**: Understanding the ResizeStatus lifecycle (Pending → In Progress → Complete/Failed) and the interaction between PVC, PV, VolumeAttachment, and the CSI driver
- **VolumeAttachment lifecycle**: How Kubernetes manages volume attachment and detachment during resize operations
- **CSI driver architecture**: Familiarity with sidecar containers (node-driver-registrar, provisioner, attacher) and their roles in volume management
- **StorageClass policies**: Understanding allowVolumeExpansion, volumeBindingMode, and their impact on PVC behavior

## Evaluation Criteria
- Ability to systematically diagnose PVC expansion issues using kubectl
- Understanding of CSI driver logging and VolumeAttachment objects
- Capability to create isolated test environments for reproducing issues
- Knowledge of safe upgrade strategies including rollback procedures and phased rollouts

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests practical troubleshooting of outbound connectivity in Kubernetes, focusing on NetworkPolicy and egress rules, which are common beginner pain points.

## Key Concepts
- NetworkPolicy and egress rules
- Outbound connectivity from pods
- kubectl inspection and targeted testing
- Minimal, verifiable policy changes

## Code Example
```javascript
kubectl get networkpolicy -A
kubectl describe networkpolicy -n default <name>
kubectl exec -it <pod> -n default -- curl -v https://payments.example.com
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate that external DNS or CI/CD proxies aren’t interfering?
- What are risks of opening egress ports broadly, and how can you mitigate them with precise allow rules?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This probes practical Kubernetes DNS troubleshooting in multi-namespace setups, focusing on CoreDNS behavior under load and real verification steps.

## Key Concepts
- Kubernetes DNS resolution and CoreDNS Corefile configuration
- Namespace isolation and DNSPolicy impact

```

```
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you distinguish DNS issues from application-layer delays?
- What metrics would indicate a CoreDNS cache bottleneck and what rollback would you perform?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical debugging of Kubernetes NetworkPolicy using kubectl in a simple front-end/back-end scenario. Evaluates ability to isolate policy issues without modifying cluster-wide security.

## Key Concepts

- NetworkPolicy basics: namespace-scoped, selectors, allowed ports
- kubectl commands: get, describe, exec, run, apply
- Pod labels and traffic paths; testing connectivity

## Code Example

```bash
# Inspect policies in the namespace
kubectl get netpol -n <ns>

# Spawn a test pod and attempt to reach the backend service
kubectl run test-pod --image=busybox --restart=Never -- sh -c "wget -qO- http://backend-svc:PORT/health || true"
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you craft a limited NetworkPolicy that only allows frontend pods to talk to backend on the required ports?
- How would you verify changes in a multi-tenant cluster with namespace isolation?

questions.question: Advanced: A 3-replica Kubernetes Deployment runs a Java app that authenticates to PostgreSQL via Vault dynamic credentials rotated every 6 hours. Propose an end-to-end, production-ready rotation strategy that avoids pod restarts, detailing: Vault config, injector/sidecar choices, how credentials are consumed, verification steps, and notification of rotation failures. Include concrete commands or manifests?

options.text: Use Vault Agent Injector to mount db credentials as a file and env var; a sidecar watches lease renewals and re-writes /vault/secrets/db-creds and signals the main process to reload (SIGHUP). TTL 6h,

Identify Pending pods and affected Deployments with kubectl get deploy -A; describe pods to confirm quota/limits; inspect MutatingWebhookConfiguration, LimitRange, and ResourceQuota in all namespaces;

Gather facts with kubectl describe pod <name> and kubectl get events; check memory requests/limits and container resources; verify probes (readiness, liveness); review sidecar logs; roll back with kub

Back up etcd, renew all certs, roll out new certs with zero downtime: etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/etcd-snap.db; kubeadm certs renew all; kubectl delete pod -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver; k

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests familiarity with production-grade secret rotation, dynamic credentials, and zero-downtime strategies in Kubernetes.

## Key Concepts

- Vault dynamic credentials
- Vault Agent Injector
- sidecar rotation pattern
- lease management and revocation
- zero-downtime reloads and readiness checks
- monitoring and alerting for Vault rotations

## Code Example

```javascript
# Example Deployment snippet (annotated for Vault injector)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: "true"
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-secret-db-creds: "database/creds/my-role"
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-db-creds: |
          {{- with secret "database/creds/my-role" -}}
          export DB_PASSWORD={{ .Data.password }}
          {{- end -}}
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:latest
        env:
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: vault-db-creds
              key: password
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test rotation in a live cluster with minimal disruption?
- What metrics and alerts would you implement to detect rotation failures?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical fault isolation of in-cluster DNS as a real beginner challenge.

## Key Concepts
- CoreDNS health, DNS resolution in-cluster, kubectl-based debugging
- Impact of DNS on service reachability and 503s
- Rollout restart as a recovery step

## Code Example
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
kubectl rollout restart deploy/coredns -n kube-system
```

## Follow-up Questions
- What alternative DNS troubleshooting steps would you take?
- How would you automate DNS health checks in a cluster?

questions.question: You're running a 3-node Kubernetes cluster with a CRD-driven AppRollout controller. You must upgrade the CRD from v1alpha1 to v1beta1 with breaking field changes and upgrade the controller image. Provide a concrete, command-driven upgrade plan including CRD validation, applying v1beta1, deploying app-ctrl:v2, a paused canary rollout, and rollback steps?

options.text: Use a 3-node etcd quorum; if a member fails and the leader is unhealthy, verify quorum with etcdctl member list, isolate writes, promote a healthy follower or replace the failed node, then restore fro

Inspect the pod’s ServiceAccount and RBAC bindings, then verify ConfigMap access. Steps: 1) identify SA from the pod: kubectl get pod <name> -n <ns> -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'; 2) test a

Plan: verify CRD conversion strategy, apply the new CRD, deploy app-ctrl:v2, create a paused canary AppRollout to validate changes, promote if healthy, otherwise rollback CRD and controller. Use kubec

Plan: check CoreDNS health, review its ConfigMap, test DNS from a pod, and roll back/fix with a minimal restart if needed. Commands: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep coredns; kubectl logs -n kub

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical upgrade expertise: CRD versioning, conversion strategy, and zero-downtime controller upgrades.

## Key Concepts
- CRD versioning and conversion strategy
- Deployment of updated controller image
- Canary rollout in a CRD-driven workflow
- Rollback procedures and safety checks
- Validation of reconciliation status and data integrity

## Code Example
```javascript
# 1) inspect conversion strategy
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfiguration -A
# 2) apply new CRD
kubectl apply -f crd_approllout_v1beta1.yaml
# 3) rollout controller image
kubectl set image deployment/appctrl-controller appctrl-controller=app-ctrl:v2
# 4) create paused canary
kubectl apply -f canary-approllout.yaml
# 5) monitor
kubectl rollout status deployment/appctrl-controller
kubectl get approllout -A
# 6) promote or rollback
kubectl patch approllout canary -p '{"spec": {"paused": false}}' --type=merge
kubectl rollout undo deployment/appctrl-controller
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you verify backward-compatibility of existing AppRollout resources after CRD upgrade?
- What metrics and signals confirm zero-downtime during promotion?

questions.question: In a Kubernetes cluster, a Deployment with 2 replicas serves a REST API behind a ClusterIP Service. options.text: Label pods with app=enricher in namespace enrichment. Create a NetworkPolicy that allows egress to api.example.com:443 from those pods (adjusting to IPs if FQDN isn’t supported). Validate with a test

Inspect app config across the pod and ConfigMap to find APP_PORT vs APP_SERVICE_PORT mismatch. Commands: kubectl get cm app-config -o yaml; kubectl describe deploy api; kubectl get pod -l app=api -o j

Key concepts: 1) ConfigMaps and environment/config files 2) Inspecting deployments, pods, and mounted config with kubectl describe/exec 3) Rollout management and verification workflows
